data.title: NLR: Participating In The Parabolic Trend
data.description:
The VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF (NYSEARCA:NLR) is trending sharply higher but is reaching a point when investors must be wondering if it is getting too frothy and whether there will soon be a "rug pull."

It makes buying NLR a challenge. This article looks at ways to participate in such an extended move and why NLR could keep charging higher.

NLR Basics

NLR is a passively managed ETF with an expense ratio of 0.56%. It tracks the MVIS® Global Uranium & Nuclear Energy Index (MVNLR) by Market Vectors, which selects stocks on the following set of rules.

  • Companies must generate at least 50% of their revenue from uranium and nuclear energy.

  • Stocks are weighted by market cap and capped at 8%.

  • Companies must have a market cap of over $150M.

  • Liquidity rules also apply.

VanEck provides further details on their page, stating NLR tracks companies involved in: (i) uranium mining; (ii) the construction, engineering and maintenance of nuclear power facilities and nuclear reactors; (iii) the production of electricity from nuclear sources; or (iv) providing equipment, technology and/or services to the nuclear power industry.

There are 28 holdings in total, with a top 10 that looks like this -

This is an international fund, with only around 50% exposure to US companies:

This introduces currency risk and country-specific risk, especially when regulations are considered.

As could be expected after a rally of over 100%, valuations are extended, and the fund has an average PE ratio of 27.3.

Of course, this doesn't tell us anything about earnings growth. Earnings could swiftly catch up and compress this margin again. Or not. It would certainly help if NLR's top holding, Oklo Inc. (OKLO), posted revenue or earnings of any kind.

Powerful Drivers

NLR has not always been a hot ETF in a hot sector. It spent around 18 years at lower highs and over a decade languishing under $60.

Powerful drivers have come to the fore in recent years, and especially 2025 as President Trump signed a series of executive orders aimed at revitalizing the nuclear industry. These orders are already bearing fruit as earlier this week, the US Army unveiled a program to build micro nuclear reactors.

As most will know, AI's huge energy demands are the main driver and have not only boosted nuclear energy, but the normally slow and steady utility sector (XLU) too.

A major shift is underway, and it's no accident that the expected demand for electricity has a similar chart to NLR and XLU.

Signs of Froth

Powerful drivers are in place and unlikely to change any time soon. The challenge for investors is knowing when the good news is priced in and when phases of the rally get too stretched and are due to snap back. For instance, the headline and text below are from September 20 and looked like a step too far towards the euphoric:

Oklo skyrockets to a new all-time high, leading a huge rally in nuclear names

Oklo Inc. (OKLO) finished Friday's trading +28.8%, posting an all-time intraday high $136.53 and soaring 63.5% for the week as the stock rode a wave of enthusiasm for the nuclear sector with a string of positive news.

OKLO dropped around 24% the following week but then rallied another 75% into this week's high.

This provides a warning: another 20-30% drop could be close.

However, it also suggests a large dip could offer a buying opportunity.

Approaching NLR

A parabolic move happens when the trend keeps getting steeper. Each of the yellow trendlines on the chart below is steeper than the one before.

In other words, the trend is curved.

This curve can easily be broken, either by a stall in the rally or a small drop below $150.

While caution is advised, given the drivers, I don't think a break would be critical to the trend or that we should be looking for a top in NLR.

That makes consolidations and corrections buying opportunities. The problem is that these are often small, and the dips rarely reach good levels. For instance, I'd like to buy at the 50-day SMA chart support, but I doubt a dip of that size will happen.

Buying at the 20-day SMA and trendline would be a more aggressive approach, but again, risks missing out.

Of course, you could buy at the current price of $163. You may suffer a drawdown if there is a dip to the trendline, but you would have to be incredibly unlucky to have bought near a top and not see an eventual profit.

The point is, there are ways to participate. Personally, I almost always buy on dips and average into a position. I would get interested around $150.

Risks

OKLO has no revenue. Another of NLR's top holdings, NuScale Power Corporation (SMR), posted revenue of only $8M in Q2. These are speculative stocks and may not live up to the hype. While NLR is diversified among 28 stocks, if the up-and-coming growth stocks fail, the whole sector will take a hit to sentiment.

There is a risk that electricity demand does not meet the project expectations. Perhaps there is a breakthrough in AI that significantly lowers energy demands. However, this looks unlikely and won't happen overnight.

I think the main risk is that the trend gets too stretched and even a minor catalyst could lead to a swift 10% drop.

Conclusions

Many will look at the YTD gains in NLR and say, "No thanks, it's moved too far." However, that means you will miss one of the strongest trends in the current market. NLR has powerful drivers and contains exciting stocks that could be key to AI energy demands. It may be stretched, but I don't think it is near the top. I am therefore looking at ways to participate, ideally by buying a dip near $150.

data.title: VBIL: Cash, But Smarter - Harvesting 0-3M T-Bill Yield With Minimal Jitters
data.description:
Introduction

The Vanguard 0-3 Month Treasury Bill ETF (NASDAQ:VBIL) is a listed fund that is designed to serve as a liquidity parking lot and thus provide an extra return on money market funds or traditional checking accounts. The particularity of this ETF compared to other ETFs is that it invests in US Treasury bills with maturities between 0 and 3 months i.e., within short-term funds. We could say that this is of ultra short duration as it invests in the shortest leg of the rate curve. These types of vehicles work very well for that purpose as they have little volatility and are very liquid.

The operation of the fund is quite easy to explain. In this case, the fund buys bills that are very close to maturity. When these bills mature, the fund collects the principal and reinvests the cash received in other bills of the same maturity. This is called roll-over, which is the practice that prevents lengthening the duration of the background and keeps the wallet glued to the short end of the curve. With this practice, stable and predictable behavior is achieved, as the ETF price hardly moves. The return comes via the interest accruing on those bills held by the holding fund and accumulates on a daily basis until it is paid out on a regular (monthly) basis to investors.

As discussed in the paragraphs above, the main component of the return on this ETF is the yield on short-term bills. Because those bills are issued at a discount to the principal and amortized at 100%, that difference becomes an income for the venturer. At present, after the September rate fall and the most likely fall in October, it is natural that income will gradually fall as a result of the renewal of those new bills issued at a lower rate.

In this type of vehicle, in addition to the above, the reader will have to keep in mind three variables that can erode (and some always do) the profitability of the vehicle. The first is obvious and is the cost, measured by the expense ratio (0.07%). The second is the potential loss of purchasing power, stemming from underlying inflation, if it exceeds the yield on bills at these maturities. If this happens, the actual carry obtained would be negative even if the nominal value appreciates. The third element is investment risk, which is what is currently happening with the declining rate environment that we have.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

In this analysis, the reader can follow two signals to assess their attractiveness at each stage of the cycle. On the one hand, the difference between the three-month T-Bill and underlying inflation, which indicates whether the bills’ profitability offsets the inflationary effect, and on the other, the slope between the 3M tranche and the Fed’s effective rate, which suggests where the vehicle’s next reinvestments will move.

In the first metric, we compare the profitability of the 3-month treasury bill regarding core inflation (Core PCE). As you can see from the graph, the blue line is inflation and the orange line is the real carry (3M – Core PCE). For an ETF like the one we are analyzing, this comparison reflects whether or not those bills are paying us above the increase in the cost of living.

After the 2022–2023 inflation peak, the Core PCE has stabilized and is in the 3% zone, while the 3M note tranche is moving close to 4.0% (anchored to the Fed’s current range). Therefore, the current line of the carry is at positive levels. The difference between what 3-month bills pay and the inflationary cost is currently 1%. This is a good score, albeit a far cry from the 3% recorded in 2023. Looking at the recent trend, we can see that this carry is gradually decreasing, driven by the recent rate cuts.

The current context of American monetary policy can help us interpret what the future holds. With FedWatch aiming for a 25 bp cut at the October meeting (98% chance), the 3M tranche will be yielding as the ETF reinvests its bills. If the Core PCE continues to fall close to 2.5% and the cuts are gradual, the real carry can remain at current levels or increase to 1.5%. In the event of an inflationary upturn (which is likely) or a more aggressive policy of cuts (which is unlikely), that carry would be compromised, potentially reaching negative levels.

Considering this analysis, I believe that for the moment, the vehicle is a good way to park liquidity, earning an extra cost of living. Something useful for the reader will be to monitor monthly the evolution of the PCE compared to the yield of the letters at 3 months.

The second metric we propose takes the shortest stretch of the curve and translates it into a language directly connected to the ETF dividend. The main line is the slope of the spread between the 3-month interest rate and the Fed’s effective rate. In a scenario where this slope is negative, it means that the market is pricing in an environment of somewhat lower rates.

The second line of the chart is the spread of the 1M–3M tranche. This line is useful to capture the immediacy of those movements and filter calendar noise in the stretch of 3M. In a vehicle that renews letters every few weeks, these two slopes can explain the rate at which the return charged by the investor is adjusted. As we can see in the graph, there are no sudden jumps, but small steps as the letters expire, which are bought back at the current levels.

The current situation may dovetail with a scenario of monetary policy loosening (FedWatch’s implicit probabilities assign a probability of nearly 100% at the next FOMC meeting). As shown in the graph, the 3M–Fed Funds line appears slightly below zero, and the 1M–3M tranche line moves at neutral levels, indicating that ETF income will gradually decrease, without a relevant impact on the price, as the duration is minimal.

Conclusion

CME’s FedWatch shows a very high likelihood of rate cuts at the FOMC meeting on October 29, so you would not expect an increase in the fund’s distributed yield, rather a gradual compression that, if inflation continues to moderate, may still leave real returns close to zero or slightly positive. With the real carry still positive (3M paying somewhat above the Core PCE) and the 3M–Fed Funds tranche sloping somewhat, the ETF continues to perform well as a “paid-for cash” with low volatility, though income will decline as the portfolio rotates. Therefore, I recommend holding the vehicle for the moment if you have it in your wallet. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Ametek: Loading Up On Shares Before Earnings
data.description:
AMETEK, Inc. (NYSE:AME) is set to report Q3 ’25 earnings on October 30, 2025, before the market open. With significant investments announced across core markets, including power generation, pharmaceuticals, aerospace & defense, and other manufacturing sectors, I believe Ametek is in a position of strength to realize an acceleration in growth over the coming years. As part of its financial discipline, Ametek remains flexible for additional bolt-on acquisitions going into the end of the year, with a potential of $4.5b in capital that can be deployed between cash and debt. I am recommending AME with a Buy rating with a price target of $220/share at 19.76x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Ametek Operations

Teeing up for Q3 ’25, Ametek ended Q2 ’25 with a book-to-bill ratio of 1x, suggesting a stable flow of backlog growth and revenue conversion; backlog in the quarter ended at $3.47b, maintaining its heightened level. For all of eFY25, management is expecting to reinvest $85mm for strategic growth initiatives, expanding its research & development and go-to-market strategy.

Ametek is realizing operating strength following the recent acquisition of Faro Technologies in May 2025, an enabler of industrial automation with machine vision and robotics offerings. I believe these technologies will be critical features when it comes to reindustrialization in the U.S. as a way for manufacturers to manage operating costs while enhancing productivity. As part of the acquisition, management anticipates the business can achieve a 30% EBITDA margin in the next 3 years, up from the pre-acquisition margin of 15%.

As part of the U.S. reshoring efforts, Ametek may benefit across a number of verticals, including pharmaceutical manufacturing and R&D investments, aerospace & defense, industrial electrification, and power generation, among others. As part of the U.S. trade deals secured with Japan and the European Union [EU], roughly $1.15T of investments will be deployed in the US through 2028 to bolster manufacturing across these sectors, potentially driving significant investments in automated manufacturing systems as well as the various manufacturing components developed by Ametek. In addition to this, roughly $350b in investments in pharmaceutical manufacturing in the U.S. have been announced across the major drugmakers, signaling growth for domestic production.

Energy may provide significant tailwinds in the coming years as utilities invest in expanding power production in support of the growing data center footprint. In total, electric utilities are expected to invest over $1.1T by 2030, a major opportunity for Ametek to service. I believe investments will largely be geared towards gas-fired power plants and renewable energy sources like wind and solar. One of the factors that may positively impact renewable energy deployment in the near term is the early sunset of the renewable energy tax credits at the end of 2027, which may result in a significant pull-forward in new capital spend for renewable power sources. Given the complexity of bringing renewable power generation online, this may lead to significant investments in grid modernization as part of the broader investment theme.

Ametek's Financial Position

Looking ahead to Q3 ’25, I’m forecasting Ametek to report $1.83b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $1.78/share, above management’s guidance. Given the strong market dynamics and significant investments being laid out for the U.S., I believe Ametek may realize an accelerated growth rate in the coming years paired with improving margins.

Ametek exited Q2 ’25 in a position of financial strength with $620mm in cash & equivalents and $1.9B in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage ratio of 0.59x net debt/aEBITDA. Management previously indicated in the Q2’25 earnings call that the firm has significant flexibility to leverage the balance sheet for more bolt-on acquisitions going forward, upwards of $4.5-5B, assuming a 2.5x leverage ratio.

Ametek experienced some working capital build in Q2 ’25, with inventories growing to 91 days on hand, up from 88 days in the prior quarter, largely driven by work in process and raw materials & purchased parts. This was likely to ensure a fluid supply chain given the uncertainty regarding the tariff environment at the time.

Risks Related to Ametek

Bull Case

Ametek will participate in multiple secular growth trends driven by foreign and domestic investments across industries. Two of the key themes Ametek will participate in are reindustrialization and power generation. Ametek remains financially flexible to engage in further M&A activity to expand its footprint in core markets.

Bear Case

Trade risk with China remains prevalent following President Trump’s announced 100% tariff on Chinese imports in retaliation for further export restrictions on rare earth elements by China. Restrictions on rare earth elements could potentially lead to delays across industries that Ametek services, particularly for aerospace & defense.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

AME shares currently trade at a modest discount to its 1-year midpoint of roughly 20.30x EV/aEBITDA, offering investors an appealing entry point for building a position. With the strong secular growth trends working for Ametek, I believe shares may realize an appealing growth rate over the coming quarters. I am recommending AME shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $220/share at 19.76x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: New Horizon Aircraft: Why I'm Investing In This Regional Air Mobility Play
data.description:
Thesis

New Horizon Aircraft Ltd. (NASDAQ:HOVR), the way i see it, is one of the most interesting new companies in aviation innovation sector.

While the investment community is focusing on popular names such as Joby Aviation (JOBY) or Archer (ACHR), which is now listed on the Nasdaq, Horizon is attempting something different.

A hybrid vertical takeoff and landing aircraft (eVTOL) that combines the advantages of a helicopter with the speed and efficiency of an airplane. The company is moving quickly from the design phase toward the creation of a full prototype, aiming for commercial use before the end of the decade.

The financial data show an organization in an acceleration stage. Losses are increasing (as I expect for a pre-revenue project), but liquidity has more than doubled within one quarter and the company’s market capitalization is beginning to reflect the future potential of regional air mobility.

The management, led by former Royal Canadian Air Force fighter pilot Brandon Robinson, presents a clear strategy, certification, technological differentiation and the creation of a dual-use model with both civilian and military applications.

The very strong multi-month momentum of the stock (+1000% this year) after its full depreciation indicates rising expectations for the company.

Who is New Horizon Aircraft?

Horizon is a Canadian aerospace company founded in 2013 and based in Lindsay, Ontario.

It specializes in developing the Cavorite X7, a hybrid vertical takeoff and landing aircraft (eVTOL).

This aircraft can transport up to seven passengers or 700 kilograms of cargo.

It flies at speeds up to 460 km/h and with a range of up to 800 km.

The aircraft uses a patented “fan-in-wing” system, where the lift fans are built inside the wings and are revealed only during takeoff and landing. Then the wings close, and the aircraft flies like a conventional airplane, which greatly reduces consumption and increases safety.

The company has already developed 50% scale working models that have successfully performed flight tests, while the full-scale prototype is currently being built and is expected to begin its first test flights in 2027.

The Company is partnering with Cert Centre Canada (3C) for certification and is supported from the U.S. Department of Defense through the High-Speed Vertical Takeoff and Landing program.

The goal is double: civilian use (air taxis, patient transport, corporate travel, logistics, rescue missions) and military utilization through variants that will not require certification by Transport Canada or the FAA.

The potential partners include industries such as Pratt & Whitney Canada. The main competitors are companies like Joby, Archer, and Lilium (OTC:LILMF) that focus on short-range electric systems.

Technology, strategy and prototype development

The Company's competitive advantage is that it is does not plan to reinvent aviation but aims to combine proven technologies within a new and innovative design.

The Cavorite X7 uses a Pratt & Whitney PT-6A class engine combined with electric subsystems.

This reduces dependence on batteries and allows operation in difficult weather conditions, one of the main obstacles for fully electric eVTOLs.

The aircraft can fly 98% of the time like a conventional airplane, while the fans are activated only for vertical takeoff and landing.

The company estimates that the cost per passenger mile will be $1.93, compared with $4.64 for a Bell 429 helicopter.

This represents a cost reduction of 58% to 64%, along with an almost 90% increase in speed.

An important differentiation factor, for me, is that Cavorite X7 could receive "Flight Into Known Icing" certification. This would allow operations in bad weather (is a prerequisite for real commercial service in countries such as Canada or Northern Europe).

Horizon expects to complete its full-scale prototype by 2026 and commence flight testing in 2027, aiming for Transport Canada certification in 2028.The strategic plan includes initial orders from rescue organizations, air service operators and military structures.

The dual use (civil and defense) offers revenue diversification and reduces certification risk, since military versions are not subject to the same regulations.

Leadership team and Collaborations

As i see it, the leadership team is one of Horizon’s main advantages. B. Robinson, the Company's CEO has deep knowledge and experience as he was a former CF-18 fighter pilot.

His brother, Brian Robinson, serves as Chief Engineer, with fifty years of experience in aeronautical engineering and a rich portfolio of patents. Brian Merker, the CFO was previously at KPMG with experience in publicly listed aviation companies.

The Company has collaborations with leading organizations (e.g., Downsview Aerospace Innovation and Research Centre) and government entities in order to take part in the INSAT initiative (Initiative for Sustainable Aviation Technology).

This is a $350 million CAD program and if approved the Company will receive a funding of 40% (for a $10.5 million CAD project).

Financial profile and capital position

Below I will analyze the financial picture of the Company, which is in a pre revenue phase with stable capital improvement.

According to the quarter results the Company reported net losses of 10.9 million CAD (almost $8 million USD) vs. losses of 2.9 million CAD last year. R&D expenses stood at $2.7 million CAD vs $0.3 million CAD last year showing how quickly technical development has picked up.

General and administrative expenses reached $3.2 million CAD increased mainly due to stock-based compensation (to employees and associates). Cash reserves rose to $16.3 million CAD (almost $11.9 million USD) vs. $7.5 million CAD last quarter (+115%).

This increase in liquidity came from the success of “Capital on Demand™ Sales Agreement” program, through which the company raised $8.3 million CAD from the issue of $3.45 million new shares.

Another $3 million CAD came from the exercise of $2.9 million warrants. The Company has the capacity to access around $14.8 million USD through the same arrangement, ensuring financial flexibility for the upcoming testing phase.

The capital structure includes 39.6 million common shares and 4,500 preferred shares Shareholders’ equity stands at $10.8 million CAD vs $2.6 million CAD in May 2025.

Equity capital increased by warrant exercises and share sales which led to a gradual recovery of a positive net position.

Horizon still has 12 million active warrants with an exercise price of $11.50 USD as well as 2.48 million stock options with an average price of $0.74 USD. The Company has no debt nor leverage.

The Company has no debt nor leverage.

The main challenge is the cash burn which stand at $2.4 million CAD per quarter.

With the cash available as of August, Horizon estimates that it can fund its business plan for at least 12 months, although beyond that period it will need to secure new capital or find a strategic partner.

Outlook and investment assessment

The market of Advanced Air Mobility -AAM- is estimated to exceed $1 trillion over the next 15 years, according to Morgan Stanley.

The Company focuses on transporting people and cargo across distances of 100 to 500 kilometers, where the solutions of small electric eVTOL are not sufficient.

Horizon presents the characteristics of an early-stage investment with significant growth potential.

It combines hybrid technology offering strong energy and efficiency benefits, serves both civilian and defense markets, maintains good liquidity with no bank borrowing and takes part in public programs that lower funding risk.

When compared to peers like Joby Aviation and Archer Aviation, Horizon’s market cap remains below 10% of the major players, but its technological risk is lower thanks to the use of mature, well-tested subsystems.

If prototype progress is confirmed during 2026, the market could revalue the stock on a much higher level.

The company’s balance sheet outlook looks also positive.

Its equity position grew more than four times within a single quarter and Horizon believes it can stay well capitalized for at least the next year.

For investors that are seeking exposure to AAM at a lower valuation and with a more diversified model, i am certain that Horizon represents an interesting long-term position. Especially if the company demonstrates faster production progress and obtains government support in 2025–2026.

The stock’s performance confirms the positive analysis above.

Last year, it has gained more than 1000% as the market begins to price in a better future for this relatively young company.

It should be noted, however, that things were not always positive since the stock began trading in January 2024 after the business combination with the former SPAC Pono Capital Three.

During the first months of trading, it lost almost all its value, falling to as low as $0.3.

Of course, it still trades well below the $10 threshold but the momentum is strong.

Finally, the stock is highly speculative as are all companies at this early stage of development, so any investor that will choose it he/she must manage the related risk carefully.

Although the chances for success are good, the risks remain real.

Risks and challenges

Horizon is in the early stages of development and is not commercially mature, leaving meaningful investment risks in place.

First, the lack of revenue and the high rate of cash consumption create the need for continuous access to capital. The share sale program and the warrants provide flexibility but too much dependence on the equity market could lead to dilution.

Second, the certification process is uncertain. Horizon is seeking approval from Transport Canada and then recognition from the FAA, but any delay could postpone commercial launch and raise development costs.

Third, the technological complexity of the hybrid system means that the company depends on important suppliers and partners. Delays in subsystem deliveries or shifts in regulations (particularly concerning hybrid fuels) could disrupt the project schedule.

In addition, the eVTOL market is extremely competitive. Joby and Archer have already advanced certification programs and access to billions in capital. As I see it, if they achieve commercial operations first, they will establish a dominant position in both network reach and brand visibility.

Finally, the Company must confront certain matters of internal governance. According to its most recent 10-Q filing, there is a "material weakness" in internal financial control due to insufficient separation of duties. The company is expected to fix this by the end of 2026 but it is still something investors should keep in mind.

The plan is realistic and the financial strategy well structured. This reduces operational risk compared to other start-ups in the sector.

Conclusion

To conclude, New Horizon Aircraft is creating in a steady way a new chapter in short-distance aviation.

The Company offers a realistic model to sustainable regional air mobility with the combination of the security of traditional airplanes and the adaptability of eVTOLs.

As we all know, every early stage project is accompanied by uncertainty and mistakes, but the strategic synergies and a powerful team can overcome these obstacles and create the circumstances of long term value.

If Horizon achieves certification until 2028 and maintains its current pace of technological progress, the stock could become one of the most attractive names in the emerging Advanced Air Mobility sector.

The stock of Horizon is for investors who are willing to wait and who are risk lovers.

(All financial figures are in Canadian dollars, using an indicative exchange rate of 1 CAD ≈ 0.71 USD. Amounts in U.S. dollars are roughly 30% lower.)

data.title: ASML: Riding Jevon's Paradox To The Moon
data.description:
Jevon’s Paradox in Full Effect

On March 21, I made the assertion that as reasoning models proliferate throughout the world and as the “cost of intelligence” declines, the demand for AI compute will not diminish but compound. Six months later, that forecast is showing its mettle through the data. Hyperscaler capex is running at record levels, data‑center construction is reaching new levels, and the one company, ASML (NASDAQ:ASML), which sits in the jaws of the various chokepoints for leading edge silicon production, has just reported back to back quarters which reflect an underlying structural demand for more and more EUV capacity. As of October 15, the paradox is now completely evident: extraordinary efficiencies in models have opened up usage, tokens, wafers. In other words, cheaper “intelligence” has not reduced but exploded the demand for compute.

From Thesis to Tape

ASML's performance over the last two quarters is evidence that Jevon's Paradox is playing out. The company reported €7.7 billion of net sales on 53.7% margins in Q2. In its just released Q3 report, the company reported €7.5 billion of net sales at 51.6% gross margins. Demand for EUV is clearly rising, despite all the doom-and-gloom that washed over the market after the release of DeepSeek-R1.

Reasoning Models Multiply Compute

Why has the per-token demand curve steepened despite the major efficiency gains? The answer is that the shift from simple pattern-matching chat to reasoning tool-using agents demand orders of magnitude more compute. Furthermore, the gains in efficiency make these tools cost-effective for a far greater range of tasks. The token price deflation is quite staggering, with a great example being OpenAI's o3 80% price cut in June. The combination of better answers at lower unit costs has spurred adoption in a growing number of industries and use-cases. This is Jevons Paradox in action.

It isn't just one vendor that is affected. China’s DeepSeek launched a worldwide price cutting war at the beginning of the year, and kept the pressure on by upgrading its reasoning R1 models and providing heavily discounted off-peak use of its system. The efficiency gains did not reduce compute demand because it freed model's to do tasks that were otherwise cost-prohibitive.

Efficiency as an Accelerant, Not a Brake

Economics textbooks state that when something becomes less expensive to use, its usage expands. This is essentially what Jevons noted in his observations of coal usage in Britain in the 19th century. Efficiency increases in steam engines (and anything else that consumes coal) meant that coal consumption expanded rather than contracted. AI is illustrating this point, although in a new way. Unlike fuel, “intelligence” doesn’t face the same human‑saturation ceiling. That is, cheaper intelligence can create new categories of demand. As such, it is not surprising that investors and operators have revived Jevons' logic in their explanations of why there is no shortage of per-token compute demand despite on model-level efficiency gains.

Indicators from the energy side support the overarching thesis. The International Energy Agency sees data center electricity use more than doubling by 2030, with AI being the driving force. Uptime Institute surveys show that AI data center availability and density issues will only get worse as operators try to accommodate for increasing AI loads. To put it another way, the price per unit is going down, but the macro load is increasing, a classic rebound effect.

The Lithography Gatekeeper

This all comes down to wafers. Advanced logic and next-gen HBM are mapped with EUV, and EUV is a one-vendor market. Reuters has noted that ASML is presently the only supplier to the world of EUV scanners and the only systems suited to economically print the most dense layers at 7nm or below for AI accelerators. It’s a monopoly largely due to the astronomically complex physics involved in these machines.

The chokepoint that is ASML's EUV machines will matter more and more as AI workloads grow. The increasingly dominant reasoning model paradigm drives far longer inference and memory footprints, which push fab geometrics and layer counts. Only ASML's machines are able to help create these increasingly complex designs. ASML's NXE:3800E, for instance delivers over 30% productivity gains over its previous generation. Simply put, the higher the EUV intensity in both logic and memory roadmaps, the greater the runway for ASML's increasingly vital tollbooth.

Hyperscaler Pull, Construction Push

Upstream, the hyperscalers are writing the checks that eventually become scanners. Microsoft told investors it will have a record capex quarter on the order of ~$30B for AI build-outs and Alphabet raised their 2025 capex plan from ~$75B to ~$85B, the bulk of which goes into data centers and servers. These are industrial scale commitments to AI infrastructure.

On the ground, data center construction is booming. A consortium led by BlackRock and including Nvidia, Microsoft and others just announced its intention to acquire Aligned Data Centers for ~$40 billion. This is the first megadeal under the recently formed AI infrastructure partnership.

Memory: The Second Demand Engine

The insatiable demand for bandwidth created by AI has turned HBM into another equal source of lithography intensity. SK Hynix, for instance, says it is done with HBM4 development and working on ramping production until 2026. Micron is stepping up U.S. investments and guiding to higher capex. Samsung’s profits are improving as tighter AI-driven memory markets lift pricing. This all translates to more accelerators and HBM, all of which inevitably leads back to lithography.

Risks Worth Watching

This thesis is not without risk. ASML has been candid that 2026 may show a flat year-over-year revenue. There is also confusion on the timing of U.S. investment given tariffs and export-control factors. Finally, there are non-trivial supply-chain inputs that are affected by rare-earth export controls. At the competitive margin, rumors persist of Chinese EUV progress. But verified reports continue to show that lithography within China itself is a long way away from high-volume EUV production. Even Canon’s and Nikon’s alternatives are complementary or auxiliary in character. So far, ASML is on the continent of EUV alone. The market weighs these as ongoing and moving inputs. Even with caution regarding 2026, the guidance given at year end has given a strong year to the stock as investors look through short-term noise to mid-term needs for AI fabrication capacity.

Conclusion

In March, I argued that AI would demonstrate Jevons’ Paradox on steroids. Each drop in the cost of “thinking” would unleash new classes of usage. Since then, model prices have fallen sharply while per token demand has increased as the same time. ASML’s numbers are the receipts. Efficiency did not kill demand but expanded and multiplied it. And because ASML remains the gatekeeper to EUV, the company continues to be the clearest “picks & shovels” beneficiary in AI.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XPLR Infrastructure: This Won't Be A Vistra, But It Has Potential
data.description:
On our last coverage of XPLR Infrastructure, LP Common Units (NYSE:XIFR) we warmed up a bit on the company. Underpinning our mild bullishness was the fact that the dividend had been removed and the income chasers were off on another venture. It was also the first time we actually owned the shares in our entire coverage history starting from September 2023. We analyzed the stock, the debt and came away with this message.

These bonds have a yield to maturity of 7.8%. But it is still a B rated credit. In a world where we are getting IG rated preferreds (with better tax rates) at over 7%, we don't see this as particularly attractive.

The other alternative is the covered call route and the premiums are quite attractive. In fact, we successfully concluded one 28% annualized return trade recently in our service. At present, the Jan 2026 $8 strikes offer a decent entry for the investor who likes to wait, but not too much.

We actually think the covered calls are more attractive on a risk-reward basis than the debt and that is what we think investors should consider.

Source: Solid Improvement But Dividend To Remain MIA

Those that played it, made a good return as the stock is now up substantially from that point and any covered call play would have done well.

We go over the latest numbers and macro developments to give you our updated thesis.

You would think XPLR infrastructure would be extremely popular these days with the assets it actually has. The third largest independent producer of wind and solar energy has not been able to jump in on the AI bubble though.

It is indeed strange that in a market that has been supply constrained and projected to get even tighter on deployment of more data centers, XPLR is failing to get any attention.

Long use power assets are in short supply and XPLR's weighted average useful life is more than two decades.

You would think there would be some sort of a bidding war here, especially with where electricity prices are running in the US.

Some might extrapolate that to mean it is a company specific problem. After all, the weakest companies buck a strong sector trend and independent power producers have positively been on fire. Even the utilities sector has been bid relentlessly.

As the recent results showed though, there isn't anything really wrong with the current results. EBITDA was flattish year over year and FCFBH (free cash flow before growth) was up 6%.

What is even more intriguing here is that XPLR has not downgraded its forecasts in recent times. That might seem like a low bar, but the company has a bad reputation for really disappointing investors. The recent trend has been a pleasant surprise.

So if you put the $600 million of FCFBG in context, it does appear really large relative to the market cap. So what gives?

The problem lies in XPLR's extremely complicated structure. The company was founded as a prayer to Income Chasers Anonymous Club. The aim was to maximize distributions and the underlying assumption from a bad management team was that interest rates will always remain low. The liabilities side was financed in a complicated manner via CEPFs. While the whole range of conditions governing them are beyond the scope of the article, what readers need to know is that they were designed to work as long ZIRP (zero interest rate policy) would continue indefinitely. As that did not happen, XPLR tanked.

The losses for investors were massive as almost everyone looked only at the yield without seeing the armada of debt/other liabilities that stood behind.

That structure is still being dealt with and we would say we are now in the third innings of this process.

Verdict

At first blush this looks like the cash flow gusher you have been dreaming of.

If you look a little deeper, you will see that it is one complicated structure and often includes language like this.

Tax credits include the removal of the pre-tax allocation of production and investment tax credits to tax equity investors less proceeds from tax equity investors, the pre-tax adjustment for production and investment tax credits earned by XPLR Infrastructure and amortization of CITC.

Source: XPLR Presentation

Well that cleared everything up.

Also, there is a reason that the company is focusing on the FCFBG metric. They are forced to repower a lot of their wind farms and that is costing them a ton of cash. This all falls under "growth" and hence maintenance capex shown above is $10-$30 million. XPLR expects to spend about $20 million for the entire year on maintenance. Actual capex though, has been moving up and hit $170 million just for the first half.

We don't think it is a bad idea to upgrade those aging assets and certainly the ROI is there in today's environment. The problem is that most investors are just tired of waiting for some sort of capital return policy and exiting. It does not help that the company's structure makes it very hard to value against traditional power producers. Our sense is that this is cheap relative to where every other power producer is trading. But that value won't flow to equity holders unless the company agrees to sell itself. In that case we can easily see it fetching $15 share. We don't currently have a position as our shares were called away. We might initiate another position in case we have a swoon.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Salesforce: $60 Billion Target Tests Investors' Memories
data.description:
Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) shares are having their best day in quite some time, up nearly 5% after setting what is reported as an 'ambitious goal' for $60 billion in revenues in FY30 (calendar 2029), with the promise of an organic revenue growth CAGR of 10%.

Perhaps I would have been more enthusiastic if it weren't for my sour memory of the previous investor day in 2022, when the company set its FY26 target at $50 billion, a mark it is set to miss by about 20%.

With shares down over 30% from the peak of the 'Agentforce Dream' hopes, I urge caution against getting overly enthusiastic and encourage investors to remain skeptical. I maintain my previous "Hold" rating.

Let's dive in.

How Salesforce Lost Investors' Excitement

If you haven't been living under a rock, you know that the biggest theme in the stock market right now is AI. For technology companies specifically, it appears that their AI positioning is almost the sole determining factor in share performance.

We have one group of companies, which we can call the clear AI winners. Those are mainly semiconductor stocks like Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), and Taiwan Semi (TSM). We have another group of companies, let's call them AI survivors, which consists of companies the market views as strong players in AI, but it does have its concerns, with names like Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOG). Lastly, there are AI losers, which is a group comprising many legacy software names, including Adobe (ADBE) and Salesforce (CRM).

Salesforce didn't always belong to the loser camp. In fact, in late 2024, after announcing Agentforce, it was viewed as one of the winners. Marc Benioff was going around dissing Microsoft's Copilot as the next Clippy and telling investors about product growth at unprecedented levels.

I argued at the peak of the Agentforce excitement that no tangible fundamentals were supporting the enthusiasm, and it turns out I was correct.

After all the talk, Salesforce delivered two quarters of single-digit growth and lost investors' excitement in the process. In fact, investors became so pessimistic that Salesforce was one of the few stocks that traded in the red the day of an OpenAI partnership announcement.

So, with that backdrop in mind, Salesforce headed into its 2025 investor day.

$60 Billion Promise Tests Investors' Memories

There are certain companies, or more correctly, management teams, that tend to overpromise. In my mind, Salesforce falls under that category. If the Agentforce example isn't enough, let's go to something very relevant to today's news, the previous investor day, which took place in September 2022.

At that investor day, Salesforce set a target for $50 billion in sales in FY26, based on the growth of its existing portfolio at the time. Well, we are in FY26 right now, and the $50 billion is nowhere in sight, inclusive of several acquisitions.

Nevertheless, Salesforce is once again guiding for a number that appears quite unlikely. The 10% organic growth target would require an acceleration from today's pace, which is a tall task for a company the size of Salesforce.

In recognition of that challenge, Salesforce has already given itself a 'grace period' of 12 to 18 months until revenue inflection. Their explanation: new products that are growing faster will take time to scale to a level that pushes the entire revenue base upwards.

In addition to faster growth, they are looking to be a Rule of 50 company by FY30. Assuming 10% revenue growth, that would mean a non-GAAP operating margin of 40%, which is more than 500 bps higher than last quarter.

That seems quite ambitious to me as well, but achievable, as they are already on pace, and they would just need to keep going.

In conclusion, I think the chances of them delivering $60 billion organically in FY30 are slim. I believe today's optimism will evaporate quite rapidly if growth doesn't accelerate in the near term.

Fundamentals Are The Only Way To Change Perception

As we've seen with other companies whose position in AI is questioned by investors, the only cure is to deliver actual results. The best example I can think of is Google. It took more than two years for Google to change investors' perception, and it wasn't something management said. It was consistent execution and results that improved from quarter to quarter, with the last straw being the acceleration in Google Search growth.

Salesforce has no way around it; the only path is through. They will have to return to double-digit revenue growth to regain investors' confidence.

In the second quarter of its fiscal 2026, Salesforce delivered constant currency revenue growth of 9%, in line with investors' expectations, similar to the previous three quarters. Growth was strongest in the Platform & Other and Analytics pillars, and weakest in Marketing & Commerce.

The operational leverage story continued, with GAAP operating margins expanding 370 bps, and non-GAAP margins up 60 bps. G&A was the main area of improvement.

They upgraded their revenue guidance for the full year, mainly due to FX, as they maintained their constant currency guide for 8% growth. On the profitability front, there weren't major changes, with GAAP metrics going down and non-GAAP metrics improving marginally.

Was it a decent quarter? Yes. Is it enough to change the perception? Evidently, no. Until Salesforce delivers double-digit revenue growth, or is at least confident enough to guide that as a near-term target, I don't believe this will change, despite today's bounce.

CRM Stock's Valuation Is The Main Reason To Be More Bullish

While my fundamental view of Salesforce hasn't changed from previous articles, one thing has changed, and that's Salesforce's valuation.

At $247 a share, Salesforce trades at 35 times forward earnings based on its GAAP guidance, which is the comparable metric to companies like Microsoft or SAP (SAP).

Of note, despite all its challenges and weaker growth trajectory, Salesforce still trades at a higher valuation compared to Microsoft based on P/E, and only slightly below SAP.

The explanation is two-fold. One, most investors are looking at Salesforce on a non-GAAP basis, even though it's a mistake in my opinion. Based on non-GAAP metrics, Salesforce appears far cheaper.

The other reason is that, unlike Microsoft, Salesforce remains a capital-light business, which justifies a higher earnings multiple, all else equal.

To me, Salesforce's valuation is now at a more reasonable level, but it's still not attractive. The metric I look at is GAAP EPS, and I can't justify paying a premium for Salesforce over Microsoft when the latter is growing twice as fast and has a far wider moat in my view, especially in the age of AI with Azure.

If Salesforce dropped below $220 a share, which would mean roughly 30 times earnings, that's where I'd consider it attractive.

Conclusion

Salesforce finally succeeded in injecting some enthusiasm among investors with a $60 billion sales target for FY30.

History tells us that they tend to shoot for the moon and not even deliver the stars, as they are on track to miss their previous Investor Day targets by nearly 20%. I'm even more skeptical in light of the recent Agentforce disappointment.

Despite significantly underperforming the market, Salesforce still trades at a rich valuation in my view, which would only be justified if it delivers revenue acceleration in the near term.

I don't see that happening and reiterate a "Hold."

data.title: Nova Minerals: One Employee, No Proven Reserves, But 'Tonnes' Of Potential
data.description:
Rare Earth Industry & Nova Minerals Overview

The race for rare earth metals has never been as hot as it is now. Last week, China announced vast new restrictions on rare earth exports, many of which are necessary for military and emerging technologies, most of which are produced almost solely in China. China has dominated the rare earth market for decades, but its edge has grown significantly over the past decade as it has led development in refining technology. However, I have covered many emerging U.S. rare and strategic/battery metal companies, and I think it's clear that U.S. soil may have comparable reserves, not fully explored because of previous financial constraints.

Throughout 2025, we've seen growing executive branch involvement in supporting the rare earth industry. Indeed, I argue this is somewhat necessary as, before recently, Chinese exports kept prices too low for profitable development. Further, the regulatory and financial barriers to entry are huge, and it may take a decade or more for exploratory projects to produce and refine rare earths profitably. Still, just because the U.S. government provides indirect or direct support for a rare earth company, that does not necessarily mean that the company's exploration and development plan will be successful.

As detailed in the bearish article on the offshore miner (TMC), "TMC the metals company: Overvalued As Exuberance Defies Economic Reality," I think the "emerging tech materials" industry has become generally overvalued due to excessive exuberance surrounding the Trump administration's support. In that article, I noted a handful of companies I've covered within this "theme": The Metals Company (TMC), Lithium Americas (LAC), United States Antimony (UAMY), American Battery Technology (ABAT), Solid Power (SLDP), NANO Nuclear (NNE), Energy Fuels (UUUU), and Oklo (OKLO). These stocks are up between 100% and over 700% this year, with huge gains since August as we're seeing more direct government funding of some of these companies.

Another interesting example that has come to my attention is Nova Minerals (NASDAQ:NVA). Nova is still smaller than most of the others, with a market capitalization of around $450M. Of course, it was worth $150M just a week ago, began 2025 at a measly $65M, and was just over $20M after going public in 2024.

So what's going on with NVA? Nova is an Australian company focused on a gold and critical minerals project in Alaska, which it owns 85% of. That sounds great on paper and has led to a massive rise in its stock price, but the market may be overlooking key risks due to excessive hype surrounding critical minerals.

One Employee and No Proven Reserves

We saw a huge acceleration in the stock on Tuesday, with a 56% gain after the U.S. ambassador to Australia asked it to brief them on its Alaskan Estelle "gold and critical minerals project". This project may be one of the most significant undeveloped gold reserves, likely holding significant supplies of other metals like antimony. Two weeks ago, the company won a $43M reward from the Department of War (renamed from the Department of Defense). From the report:

The award will enable ARR to accelerate development of a fully integrated U.S. antimony supply chain to extract, concentrate, and refine stibnite to produce military grade antimony trisulfide to assist in meeting the U.S. defense industrial base demands...

...This award will fund the initial phase of the Company’s strategy to establish a full spectrum state of the art antimony mining and refining hub based in Alaska to supply refined antimony products to the U.S. industrial base and beyond.

This comes after United States Antimony (UAMY) was awarded a $245M contract for antimony, also developing a large project in Alaska.

Nova officially has one employee (as of June 30th, 2025). This comes from its Q2 report,

As of June 30, 2025, we employ 1 employee in Australia in a corporate head office capacity, None of our employees are represented by labor unions, and we believe we have an excellent relationship with our employee.

Well, I am glad to hear that Nova is on good terms with its singular employee. It is not uncommon for pre-revenue miners to have very few full-time employees, contracting a great deal of initial labor needs out. Still, this should be noted as a risk factor in that Nova is at an extremely early stage of its development timeline. We don't even have a pre-feasibility study, though it is currently working on that, as well as environmental studies, "host rock" studies, and metallurgical studies. We know little about what is in the ground at the sites it plans to develop, nor the costs and timeline associated with development.

There is a 2023 scoping study found that "sensitivity analysis further highlighted an NPV5% of US$942M at a gold price of US$1,980 per ounce, utilizing just 23% of the resource in the production profile." That is a very early study, so its findings must be taken with a (huge) grain of salt. In my view, the discount rate used is unrealistically low, but of course, the gold margins at a $1,149 AISC (which it found in the study) are around three times higher at today's $4.2K+ gold price.

Nova is also in the process of drilling its Stibium prospect in Alaska for antimony and gold. That is not about drilling for production, but for discovering provable resources. So, if they find significant resources as inferred, we can see a lot of value in Nova, but reality may also fall widely short of expectations. Based on what we're seeing in its stock price and valuation, I think it's fair to say expectations are running very high.

Financially, Nova is about what we'd expect for a very early-stage miner. It has a small working capital position, generating small amounts of cash through external financing, which is used to offset operating cash-flow losses and investments:

If the PFS study shows a good development opportunity, we should start to see dilutions that raise capital for growth. Based on the scoping study, the net value of minerals in Estella may be very high if it truly has 9.9M ounces of gold, as that would be worth over $40B today. Add on antimony reserves, which still require more buildout of refining (with growing help from the U.S. government), and I can see how Nova's claims could easily be worth 10X its current market capitalization. That said, until the PFS study is out, truly until we see revenue ramp up, we do not know if those claims are valid. Likely, it will not be until the 2030s that Nova generates anything, given that it's still at the first stages of the exploration and development timeline.

The Bottom Line

Looking at Nova's stock volatility, it's very speculative, and that's matched by its fundamentals. It has one official employee, just a preliminary scoping study, and a somewhat vague government reward for antimony, which may or may not be found in adequate quantities in its mine. Indeed, I'd place NVA at the most end of the "risk-reward" spectrum as possible. The reward potential is there, but the risk cannot be understated.

I am neutral on NVA. I think it's a fascinating stock that is worth following, but I'd wait until the pre-feasibility study is out before taking a position. At this point, we're essentially speculating on unproven prospects in a company that is at the earliest development stage. For me, antimony is the big question, considering its price has skyrocketed with China's export ban. Because of its need for ammunition, the potential of having a large antimony reserve should bring significant government support that will reduce investment risk. But until those figures are published and we have a concrete plan for development, I'd be extremely cautious.

data.title: LVMH: Hope Is Here; More Is Needed
data.description:
Luxury fashion giant LVMH Moët Hennessy (OTCPK:LVMUY) (OTCPK:LVMHF) might have had a poor start to 2025, but the second half of the year is proving to be positive for the stock. Since I last checked in late July, it's up by 23%. It was meaningfully supported by the release of its third quarter trading update earlier this week, which saw it gain ~9% on the day.

This calls for a closer look at the update, with a view to develop a more holistic perspective on LVMH at a time when the broader conditions remain challenged.

Green shoots of turnaround...

There's certainly some good news in the update. After a YoY revenue contraction in the first two quarters of the year, LVMH finally reported a small uptick in Q3 2025. Organic revenue growth at constant currency rose by 1% YoY during the quarter, which is a welcome change from the revenue erosion seen in recent quarters. Further, a look at the details of the update underlines two specific encouraging signs, which are as follows.

  • Selective retailing sees solid pickup

Up to the first half (H1 2025), only one of the company's five revenue segments reported some growth. Of the remaining four, two were flat and the other two saw revenue contraction. In Q3 2025, however, all of them, save Fashion & Leather Goods, saw a YoY revenue expansion.

Selective Retailing is the biggest gainer of the quarter, with a 7% YoY increase. This is an important development since the segment is the biggest revenue stream for LVMH after Fashion & Leather Goods. It brought in over 21% of the revenues in the first nine months of 2025 (9M 2025).

  • Asia ex-Japan revenues pickup

From a geographical standpoint, LVMH's recovery in the Asia ex-Japan market is a sure relief, as it indicates improving trends in the biggest global luxury market, China. The geography is also the company's largest market, accounting for 27% of the revenues in 9M 2025. With its 2% YoY increase in Q3 2025, it snapped out of a notable drag in the two quarters before (see slide below).

Similarly, the turnaround in the US market is also quite encouraging given its significance for LVMH. It's right behind Asia ex-Japan, as it's the second biggest market for the company, with a 25% contribution to revenues in 9M 2025.

...but concerns persist...

However, the bigger picture is still concerning. This is not just because of LVMH's weak performance over 9M 2025 but also because of unsupportive macroeconomics and the luxury industry. Here are the two key ones:

  • Persisting softness in key pockets for LVMH

With the company's revenues having shrunk by 2% YoY for 9M 2025, it's hard to see any expansion in them for the full year 2025. Moreover, the company's biggest revenue segment, Fashion & Leather Goods, is still in contraction mode. Sure, its extent of decline reduced to 2% YoY in Q3 FY 2025 from 7% YoY in H1 2025. But considering that the segment brings in half of LVMH's revenues, this is still a somewhat worrisome sign.

Further, two of the company's key markets, Europe and Japan, are still weak. They might not be as big as the US and Asia ex-Japan markets in terms of revenue contribution, but they still bring in a substantial 26% of the total figure. Of these, the bigger European market is a particular concern, considering that both this year and the next, its GDP growth is expected to be at sub-trend levels of just over 1%.

  • Uncertain economic and industry conditions

It's hardly just Europe that's seeing weakness either. The IMF expects marginal softening in world output as such over the next two years in a "landscape marked by greater protectionism and fragmentation", the fund notes.

While the luxury market tends to be resilient even in the face of macroeconomic softness, it's unlikely to be the case this time around. In my recent article on the British trench coat brand Burberry (OTCPK:BURBY), I had pointed out a host of factors affecting the market right now (see segment on "Luxury markets remain unsupportive..." in the link).

Coupled with uncertain macros, including China's weakness, reasons including fast price rises during the recent boom years, which aren't seen as commensurate with the offerings, luxury shopping fatigue, and even luxury shame are factors affecting the market.

...and market multiples aren't fully supportive

LVMUY's market multiples aren't supportive either. I had earlier projected that the company's net income will land at $13.1 billion in 2025. If the estimate were to remain unchanged, the forward non-GAAP P/E is now at 23.72x, up from 21.71x back in July, due to the price rise in the interim. The present forward P/E is then slightly higher than the stock's five-year average of 22.05x.

However, after the latest update, the estimates do require a relook. At that time, I had assumed that LVMH's revenues would drop by 3% in 2025, in line with the number seen up to H1 2025. This drag is reduced to 2% instead, as seen in 9M 2025. The net margin kept unchanged at 14%, lower than the 14.8% seen in 2024, factoring in the potential impact of tariffs. This results in a profit figure rising to $13.6 billion and a forward P/E of 22.86x. However, even this P/E is higher than the stock's five-year average.

With 2025 almost at an end, though, it's also a good idea to make an income estimate for 2026. If revenue growth were to improve to 1%, the same as that for Q3 2025, and the net margin goes back up to 14.8%, as seen in 2024, the net income comes to $14.5 billion. This results in a forward P/E of 21.38x, which is an improvement but doesn't make a Buy case for LVMUY.

What next?

No matter how I look at it, the stock story clearly indicates that LVMH is one to Hold right now. Even as some recovery is underway, it's just too limited so far. And with the broader macro and industry conditions still uncertain, it's hard to vouch that the recovery will continue uninterrupted. Still, if the market multiples were low enough, there could be a Buy case. But even they have run ahead of themselves.

There could yet be surprises, though. For example, if LVMH sees a margin expansion or if the festive season this year turns out to be bigger than in 2024. But the trends so far don't suggest them, though a gradual recovery is possible. I'm retaining a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Bancorp: Downgrade To Hold Despite A Solid Q3 Due To Share Price Recovery
data.description:
Introduction

The super-regional bank U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) announced its results for the third quarter of 2025 today, against the backdrop of a continuing difficult macroeconomic environment and after the Federal Reserve recently lowered its benchmark interest rate by 0.25 percentage points – the first cut since December 2024.

U.S. Bancorp is one of the banks I review more or less regularly here on Seeking Alpha (overview here), the last time back in mid-April, when I upgraded USB stock to Buy, when it was still weighed down by tariff-related fears. Since then, the broader market and U.S. Bancorp have recovered, and at the time of writing, USB shares are trading at around $47, which is about 25% higher than when I published my last article.

In this brief update, I share my take on the bank's latest results and my longer-term view of the investment as part of my broadly diversified portfolio.

U.S. Bancorp Q3 2025 Earnings: Continued Solid Performance, But I Am Still Downgrading To Hold

U.S. Bancorp beat both on GAAP earnings per share and revenue consensus. Personally, I don't pay too much attention to such short-term earnings and revenue surprises, but I was pleased to see that USB's latest EPS of $1.22 (up 18% year-over-year) were not influenced by one-offs. Revenues rose by nearly 7% compared to the third quarter of 2024, to approximately $7.3 billion.

What led to these - in my view solid - results?

I would not go so far as to attribute the performance in the third quarter solely to the new CEO Gunjan Kedia, who succeeded Andy Cecere in mid-April. Although loan growth improved in the third quarter compared to both the previous quarter (+0.2%) and Q3 2024 (+1.4%) (Figure 1), these are obviously not spectacular figures and clearly indicate the ongoing reluctance for investments by companies and the private sector.

Deposits rose by 1.8% compared to the second quarter (Figure 2) and by 0.6% year-over-year. This was reassuring to see, especially given the decline in short-term interest rates and the negative trend since the fourth quarter of 2024. According to management, lower rates paid on interest-bearing deposits during the quarter contributed to net income growth, besides a favorable loan mix and fixed asset repricing.

From a longer-term perspective, however, it makes more sense to describe deposits as very stable since the acquisition of MUFG Union Bank. Given the normalization of the yield curve and the associated attractiveness of longer-term fixed-income investments, this is certainly a positive development.

Figure 3 shows a historical review of the yield curve over the last 30 months, based on daily Treasury par yield curve rates as a proxy. In addition to the increasing attractiveness of long-term fixed-income investments, the normalization tells us that U.S. banks are increasingly able to generate a positive earnings contribution via maturity transformation (explanation in this article). Combined with the general trend toward leaner and more efficient processes, this should be seen a positive sign for the longer-term earnings prospects of the entire sector (ignoring credit risk provisions, of course).

The improved operating efficiency is in part a consequence of the historical anomalies of a zero interest rate environment, and while U.S. Bancorp could have done better in recent years, we should not forget the industry-wide challenges related to personnel costs since 2022. Nevertheless, U.S. Bancorp's efficiency ratio improved by a strong 2.0 percentage points compared to the second quarter, after and already solid improvement by 1.6 percentage points in that quarter (Figure 4). In my view, this is where the new CEO's initiatives are most visible, and it is encouraging to see the bank approaching its previously stated medium-term target of 55% with good momentum.

Leaving goodwill and other intangible assets aside, USB's efficiency ratio was 55.5% for the quarter, an improvement of 2.0 percentage points compared to the previous quarter and 2.7 percentage points compared to Q3 2024. In the context of tangible equity, USB's continued solid return (ROTCE) of 18.6% in Q3 2025 is noteworthy. This is already slightly above the former CEO's medium-term target, but is not surprising given reported stable operating expenses despite strong top-line growth. The positive (adjusted) operating leverage of 530 basis points was definitely encouraging, especially since it was largely due to higher fee income and not, for example, a decline in tangible book value. In fact, U.S. Bancorp's tangible equity increased by 4.6% compared to the previous quarter and by 12.8% compared to Q3 2024.

Finally, USB's credit quality remains solid, as evidenced by the low and largely stable non-performing assets ratio (0.43% of total loans and other real estate, Figure 5) and the charge-off ratio of 0.58%, which is largely in line with the domestic average (Figure 6, third quarter number not yet published).

Against the backdrop of what I consider to be conservative lending practices and solid operating results, but also in view of the further strengthened capitalization (USB's CET1 ratio now stands at 10.9%, an increase of 30 basis points compared to the previous quarter), I consider the 4% dividend increase announced in early September to be entirely justified (not to say expected). Management also continued its share buyback program, repurchasing around 8 million shares so far this year and 2 million in Q3. Excluding shares issued for stock inventive plans and other purposes, the number of outstanding shares at the end of the third quarter of 2025 was 1,556 million, 4 million fewer than at the end of 2024.

In conclusion, I believe that U.S. Bancorp's third quarter 2025 results were reassuring from both a short-term and longer-term perspective. While the results were not spectacular, the strong growth in fee-related income, stable operating expenses, and continued stable credit quality metrics in particular confirm my investment thesis of owning U.S. Bancorp stock for the long term.

However, given the recovery in the share price by 25% since my last article and the lack of compelling arguments that would justify a more bullish stance, I will not be increasing my position in USB stock at this time. Figure 7, which illustrates U.S. Bancorp's price to book value and price to tangible book value over the past five years, shows that the shares are still trading at a small discount.

However, given what I believe to be a not fully justified broad market recovery, ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties, and an expected modest weaking in credit quality metrics due to likely higher-for-longer long-term interest rates (over which the Fed has little influence), I am downgrading USB stock to Hold. However, if volatility increases again and the premiums for USB put options with an exercise price in the low $40 range become reasonable again (i.e., an implied annualized return of 15%+), I could imagine selling a few contracts.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.
